did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed SkinScanNet, a deep learning framework using a DenseNet201 convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to classify dermoscopic images for early detection of skin cancer (specifically Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Seborrheic Keratosis) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating a deep learning framework named SkinScanNet using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy 93.4%, F1-score 92.9%, AUC 0.974), describes the methodology (DenseNet201 architecture, data preprocessing, Flask web application deployment), and compares results against baseline models (ResNet50, ViT). Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: SkinScanNet Deep Learning Framework for Early Detection of Skin Cancer from Dermoscopic Images
journal: Proceedings of the 5th International Conference on Evolutionary Computing and Mobile Sustainable Networks (ICECMSN-2025)
brief_summary: This study presents SkinScanNet, a deep learning framework for the early detection of skin cancer using dermoscopic images. The system utilizes a DenseNet201 convolutional neural network architecture trained on the HAM10000 dataset, which includes 10,015 images across six lesion types: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Seborrheic Keratosis. The model was evaluated against baseline architectures including ResNet50 and Vision Transformer variants, achieving an accuracy of 93.4%, a macro-averaged F1-score of 93.42%, and an AUC of 0.974. To facilitate clinical deployment, the framework was integrated into a Flask-based web application with real-time inference capabilities and an interactive visualization interface. The results indicate that SkinScanNet serves as a reliable clinical decision-support system for identifying malignant lesions in their initial stages.

all_task_types.target: Six clinically significant skin lesion classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Seborrheic Keratosis) and a binary malignant/non-malignant label.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multiclass lesion classification task using a DenseNet201 architecture trained on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for six lesion types. Additionally, it performs binary classification to determine if a lesion is cancerous (malignant) or non-cancerous (benign). The output includes the predicted class with the highest softmax probability, confidence scores, and probability visualizations across all lesion types.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large-scale dermoscopic image dataset containing diverse images of six primary skin lesion types, used for training and evaluation of the SkinScanNet framework.

use.training: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset using data augmentation (random rotation, flipping, zoom, brightness adjustment) to handle class imbalance and improve generalization.
use.validation: The model was validated using the HAM10000 dataset; the model with the least validation loss was selected for deployment.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 test set, achieving an accuracy of 93.4%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a measure of certainty for each prediction, visualized as probability bars in the web dashboard.
To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy and reliability of the framework in discriminating between malignant and benign lesions.
To evaluate the overall classification performance across all six lesion classes.
To measure the overall correctness of the classification model.
To evaluate the model's ability to correctly identify positive cases (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma).
To evaluate the proportion of true positive predictions among all positive predictions.
To select the best model for deployment by choosing the model with the least validation loss during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, with comparisons made against baseline models (ResNet50, ViT) based on these metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The DenseNet201 model achieved the highest performance among the compared architectures. The abstract mentions an overall accuracy of 94.86% and macro-averaged F1-score of 93.42%, but Table II and the Results section specify 93.4% accuracy and 92.9% F1-score for the test set evaluation. The model was deployed via a Flask web application with real-time inference capabilities.

conclusions: SkinScanNet, a deep learning framework utilizing a DenseNet201 architecture, achieved a classification accuracy of 93.4%, a macro-averaged F1-score of 92.9%, and an AUC of 0.974 on the HAM10000 dataset for detecting six skin lesion types.

The DenseNet201 model outperformed baseline architectures, including ResNet50 (90.2% accuracy) and Vision Transformer variants (91.8% accuracy), while requiring approximately 21% fewer FLOPs than ResNet50.
The model demonstrated high sensitivity for malignant lesions, specifically 95.7% for Melanoma and 94.2% for Basal Cell Carcinoma.
The framework was successfully deployed as a Flask-based web application with real-time inference capabilities, achieving a low latency of 28 ms per image on an NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU.

SkinScanNet addresses gaps in clinical deployability by integrating a lightweight, user-centered interface with probability visualizations, offering a trade-off between high diagnostic performance and usability for teledermatology.

The study acknowledges that existing AI models often lack generalizability across datasets and interpretability, and positions SkinScanNet as a solution that combines competitive multiclass performance with production-ready deployment.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset for both training and evaluation, with no external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The HAM10000 dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Nevus (NEV) comprising 6,705 images compared to only 216 for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) and 327 for Actinic Keratosis (ACK), which may bias model performance.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between SkinScanNet and baseline models (ResNet50, ViT); comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
There is a discrepancy in reported accuracy metrics: the abstract states an overall accuracy of 94.86%, while the results section and Table II report 93.4% accuracy on the test set.

The model was trained and evaluated on a single dataset (HAM10000), and the authors acknowledge that cross-dataset validation is necessary to address concerns regarding generalizability across different clinical settings.
The study is published in conference proceedings (ICECMSN-2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

While the system includes a Flask web application for deployment, the paper does not report clinical usability studies or user acceptance testing with dermatologists or patients to validate its practical utility in real-world workflows.

The pretraining dataset for the DenseNet201 backbone is not explicitly specified in the provided text, though it is described as 'pretrained'; this lack of detail affects reproducibility regarding the initial feature representations.
